question_title: Will France have more than one confirmed case of Ebola on September 1, 2026?
summary: France currently has exactly 1 confirmed Ebola case (a doctor returning from DRC), which per ECDC/WHO reporting has been successfully contained: self-reported at the airport, immediately isolated in a high-containment facility, clinically stable, with no secondary cases. The question resolves YES only if France exceeds 1 case before Sept 1, 2026 — requiring either secondary transmission or a second independent importation. Historical Western-country precedent strongly favors zero secondary transmission for controlled cases, and the high-risk uncontrolled-exposure window has largely passed. Residual risk comes from a rare healthcare-associated infection or a fresh aid-worker importation over the 8-week window amid the large ongoing DRC outbreak. Both pathways are low probability. Final estimate ~6%.

factors.description: France has exactly 1 confirmed case as of 9 July 2026 (ECDC/WHO). Self-reported at airport, immediately isolated in high-containment facility, described as 'successfully contained' with no secondary cases. The high-risk uncontrolled-exposure window has largely passed.

Historical base rate: across dozens of imported/evacuated Ebola cases in Western countries during 2014-2016, secondary transmission occurred only in US (2 nurses, uncontrolled ER presentation) and Spain (1 nurse). All controlled medical evacuations (incl. France 2014) had zero secondary transmission.
Second independent importation possible over ~8-week window given large active DRC outbreak (1700+ cases) and returning aid workers, but France now has heightened screening.
Bundibugyo virus; patient had only mild symptoms during flight (low early viral shedding), reducing flight-contact and healthcare-worker transmission risk.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 6% (NO)**

France currently has exactly 1 confirmed Ebola case per ECDC — a doctor returning from a DRC aid mission, who self-reported at the airport, was immediately isolated in a high-containment facility, and per reporting has been "successfully contained" with no secondary cases. 

For YES, France would need a *second* confirmed case (secondary transmission or a new importation) before September 1. Western-country precedent from 2014-2016 strongly favors zero secondary transmission for controlled cases (transmission only occurred in the US and Spain, both from uncontrolled exposure scenarios), and the high-risk window has largely passed. The main residual risks — a rare healthcare-associated infection or a fresh aid-worker importation over the 8-week window — are both low. Final probability: **0.06**.
sources_consulted: [Ebola disease outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda](https://www.ecdc.europa.eu/en/ebola-outbreak-democratic-republic-congo-and-uganda)

[The First European Ebola Case in Years Was Successfully Contained in France: What the U.S. Should Learn from That Response](https://www.medicaldaily.com/first-european-ebola-case-years-was-successfully-contained-france-what-us-should-learn-that-475984)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as **Yes** if before September 1, 2026, France has more than 1 confirmed case of Ebola in the 2026 outbreak, according to the [European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control](https://www.ecdc.europa.eu/en/ebola-outbreak-democratic-republic-congo-and-uganda) (ECDC).
fine_print: In order to streamline resolution, only reporting from the ECDC will be used for resolution.

factors.description: France has exactly 1 confirmed case as of 9 July 2026 (ECDC/WHO). Case was self-reported at airport, immediately isolated in high-containment facility, described as 'successfully contained' with no secondary cases. The high-risk uncontrolled-exposure window has largely passed.

Historical base rate: across dozens of imported/evacuated Ebola cases in Western countries during 2014-2016, secondary transmission occurred only in US (2 nurses, from an uncontrolled ER presentation) and Spain (1 nurse). All controlled medical evacuations (incl. France 2014) had ZERO secondary transmission. This is a controlled case.
Second independent importation possible over ~8 week window given large active DRC outbreak (1700+ cases) and returning aid workers, but France now has heightened screening; low probability.
Bundibugyo virus, patient had only mild symptoms during flight (low viral shedding early), reducing flight-contact and healthcare-worker transmission risk.

input.anchor: ~10% base rate for a single controlled imported Ebola case in a Western country leading to a second case (secondary transmission or new importation), derived from 2014-2016 precedent where controlled evacuations had ~0% secondary transmission but overall importation pressure exists.
input.assessment: Question resolves YES only if France exceeds 1 confirmed case (secondary transmission OR a second independent importation) before Sept 1. Current status: 1 case, contained, isolated, stable, no secondary cases. Western-country precedent strongly favors zero secondary transmission for controlled cases. Main residual risks: a healthcare-associated infection (rare in high-containment facilities) or a fresh importation over the 8-week window. Both are low. Estimate ~6%.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether ECDC would count a flight contact or healthcare worker infection; whether another aid worker importation occurs before Sept 1. Both low probability.
input.tool_audit: research() sub-agent timed out (100000s) - failed. web_search and fetch_url worked well, confirmed ECDC shows only 1 France case and 'successfully contained' reporting.
input.process_reflection: The research() tool timeout was a significant friction point - it failed entirely and I had to fall back to direct web_search/fetch_url, which worked fine. For a straightforward epidemiological containment question, direct search was sufficient and faster.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All four factors are grounded in the research trace, with correct logit directions, and the forecast is well-calibrated.

FACTOR VERIFICATION:
- Factor 1 (1 confirmed case, self-reported at airport, high-containment isolation, "successfully contained," no secondary cases): Fully supported. ECDC fetch confirms 1 case and no secondary transmission; the medicaldaily.com article confirms "successfully contained," self-reporting at the airport on June 23, and transfer to a "high-containment healthcare facility." Negative logit correctly reduces YES.
- Factor 2 (2014-2016 base rate; US 2 nurses, Spain 1 nurse, zero transmission in controlled evacuations): This is reference-class/base-rate reasoning rather than a specific event claim. The core conclusion (controlled cases rarely transmit) is consistent with the "successfully contained" reporting in the trace. Acceptable as base-rate evidence, not a hallucination.
- Factor 3 (second importation possible; DRC outbreak 1,700+ cases): Supported — AZERTAC/globalsecurity "tops 1,700 confirmed cases" and ECDC "1,708 confirmed cases." Direction correct.
- Factor 4 (Bundibugyo strain, mild/asymptomatic during flight, low early shedding): Supported — Wikipedia confirms Bundibugyo; medicaldaily/euronews confirm "almost asymptomatic," only headaches.

PRE-PUBLICATION EVENT: The index case (June 24) precedes publication (July 8), but the question asks whether France has MORE THAN 1 case. The single index case is the baseline, not a YES resolution. The agent correctly treats the question as forward-looking (requires a genuinely 2nd case via secondary transmission or new importation). No Case-2 violation.

RESOLUTION ALIGNMENT: Criteria designate ECDC as the sole authoritative source. The agent fetched the ECDC page directly (twice) and confirmed 1 case / zero secondary. Well-aligned with the specific resolution source.

TOOL AUDIT: Accurate. Agent states research sub-agent timed out and web_search/fetch_url worked — matches ground truth (research 100% error, fetch_url and web_search 0% error).

PROBABILITY: Agent notes factor sum implies ~2% but sets 6% to preserve allowance for two independent risk paths over the 8-week window. This is a defensible upward adjustment (not double-discounting) given 1,700+ active DRC cases and aid-worker rotation. My independent estimate lands in the ~4-10% range for a recovered/discharged controlled case with zero secondary transmission plus modest fresh-importation pressure. 6% sits well within this; no meaningful divergence.

COUNTERARGUMENT: Strong — constructs a coherent alternative (importation pressure, aid-worker rotation to France, US/Spain healthcare-worker precedent, cumulative two-month window) grounded in trace evidence.

ANCHOR: 10% → 6% is a small, downward, well-justified departure (the specific patient already recovered/discharged with zero secondary cases). Self-confidence of 0.82 is reasonable given strong direct ECDC confirmation balanced against 8-week residual uncertainty. No errors found.
